chapters.narration: The navigation principles work in forests, skies, and abstract worlds. A vector space is any collection where you can add vectors and scale them — and the rules always behave. Addition is commutative and associative. Scalar multiplication distributes. There's a zero vector and every vector has an inverse.

R two and R three are vector spaces. So are polynomials, matrices, and functions — anything where addition and scaling make sense. A two-by-two matrix space has dimension four. Any matrix is a combination of four basis matrices. Same rules, different objects.

A subspace is a vector space sitting inside a larger one. Add two vectors in the subspace, stay in the subspace. Scale one, still inside. The span of any vectors is a subspace. The null space and column space of a matrix are subspaces too.
